occurrenceRemarks: Rescued from pool, photos taken as it was drying off

I have observed a number of these small mining bees on feeding on Daisies. Probably a male based on the following information. "The four dark patches on the thorax (made of pads of mossy hairs; two on mesonotum and two on metanotum) are distinctive for the male of this species."
On a Chaparral Mallow (Malacothamnus fasciculatus). Mission Trails Regional Park, San Diego, CA OLYMPUS DIGITAL CAMERA P5310546
A tiny bee that kept flying around and coming back to rest briefly on this same rock
I observed this Calliopsis bee having lunch with a Crab Spider on a Daisy.

Over several minutes 2-3 individuals would come and land on this paving stone for 1-5 seconds before taking off again. A few times 2 individuals would brief interact, bot most times the bee was alone. Presumably patrolling males?
On a Wild Morning Glory (Calystegia macrostegia) Mission Trails Regional Park, San Diego, CA OLYMPUS DIGITAL CAMERA P5111005
~ 5 mm bee on small garden flower (Dahlberg daisy)
About 5 mm long. 3-5 of these chased each other around the rock.

Calliopsis rhodophila is a membrane- winged insect of the Andrenidae family . The scientific name of the species was first validly published in 1897 by Cockerell. [1] C. rhodophila - polylectic bee, though frequently collects pollen in Mallows.
